coredata.dc:title: One step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots/hydroxyapatite:Eu,Gd composite with dual-emissive and solid-state photoluminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Qing
Xu, Wei
Chen, Qianqian
Xue, Chaorui
Li, Ning
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon dots/hydroxyapatite:Eu,Gd (N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd) composite was synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal route. The obtained composite exhibited porous structure which played key roles in inhibiting the aggregation of N-CDs and enhancing the light absorption. Interestingly, the photoluminescence performance of N-CDs and HA:Eu,Gd was maintained after the one-step in-situ synthesis. Thus, N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd exhibited dual-emissive and solid state photoluminescence under single wavelength excitation. The unique optical features could endow N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd potential applications in ion detection and other fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Eu/Gd co-doped hydroxyapatite
Nanocomposites
Dual-emission
Solid-state photoluminescence

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and (B) HRTEM image of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd.
(A) FTIR, (B) UV–vis absorption spectra and (C) XRD patterns of as prepared samples.
(A) PL emission spectra of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd excited at different wavelengths. (B) PL decay spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd.
(A) PL responses of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd in the presence of different metal ions (λex = 395 nm, [Mn+] = 1 mM, pH = 7.0). (B) Variation of PL intensity of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd with the concentrations of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: One step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots/hydroxyapatite:Eu,Gd composite with dual-emissive and solid-state photoluminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xueyuan Road 3, Taiyuan 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xueyuan Road 3, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tsinghua University, State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tsinghua University, State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon dots/hydroxyapatite:Eu,Gd (N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd) composite was synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal route. The obtained composite exhibited porous structure which played key roles in inhibiting the aggregation of N-CDs and enhancing the light absorption. Interestingly, the photoluminescence performance of N-CDs and HA:Eu,Gd was maintained after the one-step in-situ synthesis. Thus, N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd exhibited dual-emissive and solid state photoluminescence under single wavelength excitation. The unique optical features could endow N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd potential applications in ion detection and other fields.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new class of carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDs) have received increasing attention owing to their unique optical properties, low cost, low-toxicity, chemical stability, and ease of functionalization. To date, CDs have exhibited great potential for various applications including sensing [1], catalyst [2], solar energy usage [3–7], and energy storage [8,9]. However, the photoluminescence (PL) of CDs is usually in the blue or green region and easily quenched in the solid state, which largely restrict their wider applications. To solve these disadvantages, many efforts have been made to tune the PL properties of CDs [10–17]. For example, Sun’s group prepared multiple color emissive CDs by thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and urea [13]. Qu and co-workers have synthesized CDs with near-infrared absorption and enhanced near-infrared PL [14]. Liu et al. obtained nitrogen-doped CDs with solid state photoluminescence property [15]. Moreover, Lu et al. demonstrated pressure triggered aggregation-induced emission enhancement in red emissive CDs [18]. Our group also synthesized CDs-in-SBA-15 fluorescent powders by encapsulating CDs in the mesoporous channels of SBA-15 [19]. However, CDs with multicolor emissions in the solid state are still highly desired since numerous applications need multicolor emissive solid materials such as light-emitting diodes, photoluminescence sensors. Now there are a few reports on constructing solid CDs-based composites with multicolor emission. A dual-emissive CDs/metal-organic framework was achieved by incorporating yellow and blue emitting CDs into ZIF-8 [20]. Recently, the CDs-based composites containing europium (Eu) have been developed given the red-emission property of Eu ions [21–24]. However, the preparation process for such composites is complicated. Therefore, it is necessary to develop CDs-based composites with expected optical performance via cost-effective and simple routes.

In view of the luminescence properties of hydroxyapatite (HA) doped with rare earth elements, plus the advantages of HA such as high stability, excellent biocompatibility, green and environmental friendliness [25,26], in this study, we synthesized a novel composite consisting of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) and Eu/Gd co-doped hydroxyapatite (HA:Eu,Gd) via a one-step hydrothermal method. The proposed strategy simplified the synthesis procedure and effectively reduced the cost. The obtained N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd possessed porous structure which not only suppressed the aggregation of CDs but also led to a significant increase of UV–vis light absorption. More excitingly, the PL property of N-CDs and HA:Eu,Gd have been kept after their combination through the facile one-step in-situ hydrothermal synthesis. Thus, N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd exhibited dual-emissive and solid-state luminescence property under single wavelength excitation. Such unique optical features could make N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd be a promising candidate in numerous applications such as sensors, optoelectronic devices and other fields.

N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd was synthesized hydrothermally by heating a mixture of sodium citrate, diethylenetriamine, calcium nitrate, diamine hydrogen phosphate, europium nitrate and gadolinium nitrate. Here, sodium citrate was used as both carbon source for synthesis of CDs and structure regulating agent of HA. Diethylenetriamine was selected as nitrogen dopant source. In addition, gadolinium (Gd) was introduced to strengthen the red emission from europium (Eu) [25].

Fig. 1 shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd. It can be seen that HA exhibited short rod like structure, and many pores were distributed in the particles. The mesoporous structure was corroborated by nitrogen sorption isotherms and pore size distribution. As shown in Fig. S1, the composite displayed a type IV isotherm resulting from the formation of mesopores with pore size in the range of 4–27 nm. The specific surface area of the composite reached to 87.6 cm3 g−1. Suggested by the reference [27], sodium citrate contributed to the formation of porous structure. More interestingly, the HRTEM image in Fig. 1B illustrated the presence of carbon dots. And two types of lattice space of 0.22 and 0.28 nm were observed, corresponding to (1 0 0) plane of CDs and (2 1 1) plane of HA, respectively.

To further investigate the composition and structure of the obtained composite, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) and UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out. In the FTIR spectra shown in Fig. 2 A, N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd exhibited broader and sharper peaks at about 3160–3670 cm−1 and 1340–1770 cm−1 in comparison to HA:Eu,Gd. The former was attributed to the OH (3450 cm−1) and NH (3260 cm−1) stretching vibrations, and the later was mainly ascribed to CO/CN (1640 cm−1) and CN (1450 cm−1) stretching vibrations [28,29]. This further confirmed the formation of N-CDs. Fig. 2B shows the results of UV–vis spectra of the as prepared samples. It is seen that N-CDs showed absorption at about 240 nm and 340 nm, which was assigned to the π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transition of CO and CN/CN [30], respectively. For HA:Eu,Gd, besides the absorption range at 200–280 nm corresponding to the wide bandgap of HA, another absorption peak at around 395 nm appeared as indicated by the arrow (the inset in Fig. 2B), which belonged to the intrinsic 4f-4f transition absorption of Eu [31]. This signified that Eu was successfully doped into HA. Compared to single N-CDs and HA:Eu,Gd, it was found that the light absorption of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd was significantly strengthened and the band edge greatly shifted in the long wavelength range to 550 nm. The reason for the improved absorption performance could be explained by the porous structure which led to multiple reflection of light so as to increase the UV–vis absorption [32]. In addition, it was observed that the absorption band corresponding to the n-π* transition of CO/CN in the N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd shifted from 340 nm to 310 nm in compassion to N-CDs. Such phenomenon was partially associated with the interaction between N-CDs and HA [33], to some extent, which changed the surface states of N-CDs in the N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd composite. Note that the crystal structure of HA has not been changed despite the occurrence of the interaction. This was supported by XRD patterns shown in Fig. 2C, where N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd exhibited a typical XRD pattern of HA (JCPDS, 009-0432). On the basis of the characterizations above, it can be concluded that N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd was successfully obtained.

Fig. 3 and S2 show the photoluminescence properties of the prepared samples. N-CDs showed blue emission (Fig. S2A), and the maximum emission peak at ca. 457 nm did not shifted when the excitation wavelength changed from 310 to 400 nm. The excitation-independent property implied the surface state of CDs was uniform that resulted in the same energy level so as to form a single luminescent center. This was consistent with the result reported in the literature [30]. As shown in Fig. S2B, when the optimal excitation wavelength of 395 nm was employed, HA:Eu,Gd exhibited more intense emission centered at 457, 595, 621 and 702 nm, which could be attributed to the energy level transitions of Eu ions. More importantly, it is seen from Fig. 3A that the PL spectra of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd simultaneously showed the emission from N-CDs and HA:Eu,Gd under single wavelength excitation. Furthermore, the PL emission spectra showed almost no shift when excited by different wavelengths, exhibiting the excitation-independent emission property. Such results implied that the electronic structures of N-CDs and Eu ions were not significantly influenced during the synthesis process, although a certain interaction between N-CDs and HA:Eu,Gd occurred. Therefore, the PL emission property of both N-CDs and HA:Eu,Gd have been preserved. Moreover, the unchanged PL property of N-CDs was also associated with the porous structure of HA that made N-CDs disperse uniformly in HA, as revealed by the HRTEM image (Fig. 1B), so as to obstruct their self-quenching. Fig. 3B shows the PL decay results of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd and N-CDs. It is seen that the PL lifetime of both N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd and N-CDs were shown a single exponential decay, indicating that the surface state of N-CDs in the composite was similar with that of single N-CDs. Besides, a little difference was observed that the lifetime decreased from 11.7 ns in N-CDs to 5.6 ns in N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd. This could be interpreted by the fact that the formed interfacial bonding between N-CDs and HA:Eu,Gd owing to their reaction facilitated the transfer of photogenerated charges, thus leading to a reduce of the lifetime. It is worthwhile to mention that because of the well-contacted interface, the obtained N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd composite exhibited good stability. The PL property still remained even after being stored for several months in ambient conditions.

To demonstrate the superiority of the synthesis strategy, several control experiments were designed as follows: one was to prepare CDs/HA in the absence of diethylenetriamine and rare earth ions using the same fabrication process with N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd; the other was to synthesize N-CDs and HA:Eu,Gd firstly and then combined them via the hydrothermal treatment at low temperature 140 °C similar with the method reported in our previous work [33]. The obtained sample was labeled as N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd-C. As shown in Fig. S3A, the obtained CDs/HA showed blue emission, however, the PL spectra shifted under different excitation wavelength. This manifested that diethylenetriamine played key roles in tailoring the surface states of CDs, one was used as the N dopant source, the other was acting as the passivation agent, both of which contributed to the excitation-independent PL property of N-CDs [30]. For N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd-C, the PL spectra only showed red emission from the f-f transitions of Eu ions (Fig. S3B). This is likely because the interaction between N-CDs and HA under the ex-situ synthesis condition changed the surface passivation state which gave rise to the PL quenching of N-CDs. Hence, the one-step in-situ synthesis displayed its obvious superiority in obtaining N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd with dual emissive and solid-state PL property.

To gain further insight into the PL performance of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd, we explored the PL responses of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd in the presence of metal ions. All the PL spectra were measured under excitation wavelength of 395 nm. Here, the ratio value of [(I457/I621)/(I457/I621)0] was used to exhibit the effect of different metal ions on the PL intensity, where (I457/I621)0 and I457/I621 were the ratio of PL intensity of N-CDs at 457 nm to PL intensity of HA:Eu,Gd at 621 nm before and after the addition of metal ions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, it was observed that I457/I621 exhibited an obvious decrease in the presence of Fe3+, while the ratio value showed little change in the presence of other ions, demonstrating the potential of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd for selective detection of Fe3+. Fig. 4B shows the PL variation of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd with the concentrations of Fe3+. It was found that the PL intensities of both CDs and HA:Eu,Gd decreased with increasing the concentrations of Fe3+. But interestingly, the ratio value of I457/I621 showed a linear increase over the low concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–60 μM, while it linearly decreased over the higher concentration of Fe3+ ranging from 60 μM to 2 mM, as shown in Fig. S4. This indicated that the degree of PL quenching of HA:Eu,Gd was greater than that of N-CDs when adding low concentration of Fe3+. Conversely, when increasing the concentration of Fe3+, the PL quenching of N-CDs became more stronger in comparison to that of HA:Eu,Gd. Such phenomenon could be understood as follows. At low concentration, the interaction between Fe3+ and HA:Eu,Gd was predominant due to the high specific surface area of HA:Eu,Gd. With the increase of the concentration of Fe3+, the interaction between the carboxyl/amino groups on the surface of N-CDs and Fe3+ became stronger, thus leading to a significant PL quenching of N-CDs [24,34,35]. The results indicated that, at least partly, the potential use of the dual emissive of N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd as a ratiometric probe in ion detection, but more studies are still needed to achieve a meaningful result.

In this work we prepared a new N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd composite by one-step hydrothermal method. This method had the advantages of easy operation, good repeatability and low cost. The resulting N-CDs/HA:Eu possessed porous structure and high specific surface area, which effectively inhibited the aggregation of CDs and led to a significant improvement of UV–vis light absorption. More importantly, the photoluminescence properties of both N-CDs and Eu ions have been preserved in the obtained composite. As a result, N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd showed dual-emissive and solid-state photoluminescence under single wavelength excitation. The unique PL performance could make N-CDs/HA:Eu,Gd potentially useful in various application such as ion detection and other fields.

Qing Chang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Wei Xu: Validation, Investigation. Qianqian Chen: Investigation, Visualization. Chaorui Xue: Methodology. Ning Li: Methodology. Jinlong Yang: Resources. Shengliang Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51502270, U1510125, 51602292), Shanxi Provincial Foundation for Youths and International Cooperation Project (201801D221085, 201803D421091), Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi (OIT), Program for the Young Academic Leaders and Scientific Innovation Team of North University of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.144862.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, J.S. Wei, H.M. Xiong, Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence, Nanoscale 6 (2014) 13817-13823.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-colour carbon dots: from energy-efficient synthesis to concentration-dependent photoluminescence properties
Ceram. Int.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Meng, Q. Chang, C. Xue, J. Yang, S. Hu, Full-colour carbon dots: from energy-efficient synthesis to concentration-dependent photoluminescence properties, Ceram. Int. 53 (2017) 3074-3077.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tailoring color emissions from N-doped graphene quantum dots for bioimaging applications
Light. Sci. Appl.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Qu, M. Zheng, J. Li, Z. Xie, Z. Sun, Tailoring color emissions from N-doped graphene quantum dots for bioimaging applications, Light. Sci. Appl. 4 (2015) e364.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facilely prepared carbon dots and rare earth ion doped hybrid composites for ratio-metric pH sensing and white-light emission
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Zhang, Y. Zhai, H. Wang, J. Zhu, L. Xu, B. Dong, H. Song, Facilely prepared carbon dots and rare earth ion doped hybrid composites for ratio-metric pH sensing and white-light emission, RSC Adv. 6 (2016) 61468-61472.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective breaking of hydrogen bonds of layered carbon nitride for visible light photocatalysis
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Kang, Y. Yang, L.C. Yin, X. Kang, L. Wang, G. Liu, H.M. Cheng, Selective breaking of hydrogen bonds of layered carbon nitride for visible light photocatalysis, Adv. Mater. 28 (2016) 6471-6477.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydroxyapatite supported N-doped carbon quantum dots for visible-light photocatalysis
Mater. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Chang, K.K. Li, S.L. Hu, Y.G. Dong, J.L. Yang, Hydroxyapatite supported N-doped carbon quantum dots for visible-light photocatalysis, Mater. Lett. 175 (2016) 44-47.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of highly photoluminescent carbon dots via citric acid and Tris for iron(III) ions sensors and bioimaging
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhou, Z. Zhou, A. Gong, Y. Zhang, Q. Li, Synthesis of highly photoluminescent carbon dots via citric acid and Tris for iron(III) ions sensors and bioimaging, Talanta 143 (2015) 107-113.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for the fluorescent detection of metal ions
Biosens. Bioelectron.
